chapters.title: The invisible presence of infidelity
chapters.text:
At this very moment, somewhere, someone is either cheating or being cheated on. Unfortunately, infidelity is a part of life that could potentially affect anyone’s relationships, whether it’s yours or those of your friends and family members. The curse of cheating is as widespread as it gets, but it remains a societal taboo; it is rarely talked about, shrouded in mystery and gossip. Attitudes toward affairs vary worldwide ; some cultures frown upon them, while others consider affairs inevitable and natural. They all, however, agree on the same thing — people cheat because their relationships are unsatisfactory, and their love life leaves much to be desired.

Infidelity is not the first crack in the marriage or the first sign that things are not going right. It is a dramatic manifestation of ongoing issues, such as tension, silence, or miscommunication. When infidelity occurs, it can lead to difficult discussions that, in some cases, result in divorce. A socially acceptable way of dealing with this situation is to part ways, move out, and burn a couple of pictures. But what if there is a way to save the relationship? What if infidelity is not the end but a new milestone for transformation, healing, and love?

Infidelity is a mirror reflecting people's insecurities. Understanding it means understanding human nature.

It is high time to put the existing stereotypes about unfaithfulness on trial. Betrayal is inevitably painful. However, for some, understanding the reasons behind it might provide a degree of comfort or aid in processing the situation. It’s time to get into a broader conversation that will help you define cheating, analyze its causes, learn how it can affect the people involved, and understand how they might grapple with its consequences.

chapters.title: What makes a marriage fragile?
chapters.text:
There is a general belief that infidelity is something only morally corrupt people do. However, since betrayal is an enduring topic frequently discussed, it seems pervasive. Infidelity is much more complex; it directly correlates with marriage, monogamy, and how these notions evolved. In the past, people viewed marriage as purely economic and political; it was a forced and arranged unity of two people. As people see it now, the idea of intimacy was unheard of in the past. The main marriage topics were kids and money; the more, the better. Affairs, on the other hand, were profoundly romantic and brave. It was the only way for many to find true happiness and experience love.

Thankfully, times have changed, and so has the concept of marriage. It is no longer a partnership void of feelings nor a trap. Nowadays, love has become an essential part of the marital equation. Simultaneously, women are breaking through rigid gender roles, and the focus of sexual activity is shifting from procreation to personal pleasure. Modern people also expect different things from marriage; everyone wants their spouse to become everything at once — a lover, friend, cheerleader, and business partner. At the same time, infidelity has started carrying negative connotations in society.

It seems like marriage is the perfect place to be; it combines passion, suppressed in the past, and stability. This union should be successful, yet people constantly break their vows. The sad truth is there are higher chances of failure wherever feelings and vulnerability are involved. In this way, modern marriages lose to those organized in the past. Moreover, all these high standards and expectations for the partner to fulfill every need might pile up on a person; they will unavoidably crumble under the weight of such demands.

Sadly, people's attitudes toward goods and services in capitalist society have entered their relationships. Their expectations and demands rise as soon as they get what (or who) they want. They strive for a more romantic, passionate, and adventurous experience. Why should they settle for this perfectly acceptable option if someone somewhere has something slightly better to offer? After all, in consumerism, novelty is key.

Nowadays, people divorce or cheat because they could be happier with another person.

chapters.title: Infidelity checklist
chapters.text:
What percentage of people cheat? Before answering this seemingly simple question, one should define infidelity, which is a tricky task. Is speaking to someone online cheating? And what about porn, dating apps, open relationships, or fantasies about a colleague? There isn’t a unanimously agreed-upon definition of infidelity, and because of this, the percentage of cheaters may vary significantly.

In the past, religion, law, and tradition dictated the boundaries and ethics of relationships. But nowadays, society has removed all these factors. Who then draws the lines and decides what is wrong and right? This freedom calls on couples to set their terms, starting with honest conversations about what each partner considers an affair and betrayal of trust. Though awkward and challenging, such discussions will erase the topic's uncertainty.

It is easier to navigate difficult moments if partners are on the same page about their relationship.

There is a way, however, to establish some common signs of unfaithfulness. People's understanding of infidelity comes from their personal experiences or what they've heard from their friends and relatives. Most likely, all these stories share typical elements, such as:

• Secrecy — Whatever form it takes, an affair is always shrouded in secrecy. It brings the feeling of thrill and excitement for the cheater.
• Sexual alchemy — Affairs also include a specific sexual element but not necessarily sexual intercourse; it is more about desiring something forbidden.
• Emotional involvement — Often, feelings of love and affection people experience in their affairs can overshadow those in the “legal” relationship.

The lack of precise definition isn’t the only factor that hinders data collection and cheating statistics ; people are rarely truthful when answering questions about their sex lives. Even the promise of complete anonymity doesn’t guarantee genuine responses. Therefore, analysts estimate that, in the United States, the rate of cheating fluctuates between 26 percent to 70 percent for women and 33 to 75 for men.

When it comes to sex, people lie — especially about sex they are not supposed to be having. ~ Esther Perel

Did you know? There are nine countries where women can face the death penalty for cheating.

chapters.title: The emotional toll of infidelity
chapters.text:
Another question often arises in infidelity conversations: "Why does it hurt so much?" There aren’t many things that can shatter a relationship so irrevocably. An affair is a betrayal on multiple levels; it brings deceit, rejection, humiliation, and abandonment. The usual response to disclosing an affair is absolute shock, which launches the initial response: fight, flight, or freeze. After this reaction follows a post-affair recovery comprising three phases:

• The crisis is a difficult period. Everyone involved has intense emotions that they must process in a safe, non-judgmental environment.
• Meaning-making is the time to explore the reasons behind an affair and other intricate details to help both partners understand what happened.
• Visioning is when couples must answer difficult questions about what comes next and whether they will continue together or separate.

The first phase is the most challenging because the couple feels fully isolated. It happens due to the stigma that surrounds infidelity. How can a hurt couple begin an honest conversation with friends and family if they know pity and judgment are the only reactions they’ll face? An experienced couples’ therapist can become the much-needed emotional support in this situation. They can provide a dual perspective encompassing the different experiences of both individuals involved.

Infidelity is so painful because it directly attacks everything people know and love.

To deal with emotions that arise after discovering an affair, one must know how to make sense of them. Not only does it affect a person’s future, but it also transforms how they view the past. The betrayed person often begins questioning every aspect of the relationship — was any of it even real? Where amidst the couple’s happy memories was another person hiding?

We are willing to concede that the future is unpredictable, but we expect the past to be dependable. ~ Esther Perel

Infidelity often means not only a loss of love but a loss of self. It gives way to self-doubt and confusion, making the betrayed, but more often the cheater, reevaluate who they are. The discovery of an affair and the pain they see in the eyes of the betrayed partner is a shock that makes the cheater confront a new self-image that does not match the one they had of themselves.

chapters.title: Making sense of betrayal
chapters.text:
Self-blame is the most common reaction of people who have learned about cheating. They start questioning what they could have done wrong to make their partners look for satisfaction in a different place. These individuals often grapple with a silent suspicion that they are living a lie. They might question their decisions, seeing them as potential mistakes. They may also feel like there is something vital they overlooked or failed to explore. This curiosity-infused doubt drives them to the edge. But what if it had nothing to do with them? Even people in happy, stable, and fulfilling relationships can cheat, so connecting the affair to the personal qualities of a betrayed person or marital issues might be wrong.

Conversations about affairs must include both sides of a story to move past the hurt and draw helpful conclusions.

Among the most common reasons for cheating is the need for self-discovery. Often, an affair can become the much-needed impulse that triggers a quest for a new identity. They can help people grow, transform, and explore. However noble the reasons behind infidelity are, the pain will still be agony to the betrayed person. Sometimes, when people look for another lover, they turn away not from their partner but from a specific version of themselves. So, an affair can signify an identity crisis and the need for immediate change within oneself. For example, some women who are dutiful wives and loving mothers find it exciting to break free from this image. Even for a short period, they want to revive this feeling of high school love or a forbidden romance with someone they would never marry.

When two people are having an affair, they thrive in this mysterious, lowkey relationship. They may fantasize about what they might do when the truth comes out and how they finally get to be in public together. But as practice shows, affairs (or relationships that stem from them) do not last long when the romance comes to light. The initial excitement fades away; the relationship loses its primary purpose and becomes routine, something they have been running from all this time. That's why many choose to break up.

chapters.title: A way out of the ruins of a relationship
chapters.text:
When the initial emotional response fades away, and the meaning-making process is over, then comes the dreaded time of answering a simple question: “What comes next?” Though betrayal hurts, it doesn’t mean ending the relationship is the only way to deal with it. “Once a cheater, always a cheater” sounds catchy, but it is far from the truth.

If the reasons behind infidelity vary, shouldn't the outcome also be diverse? The betrayed partner isn’t always innocent in a relationship. They might engage in emotional or physical abuse, leading their partner to seek love, acceptance, and refuge elsewhere. Countless reasons and situations push people toward infidelity, so why narrow it down to something trivial, like lust or boredom?

The affair may transform a relationship, destroy or sustain it, or create something new. The direction, for worse or better, is up to those involved. There are a lot of people who choose to work things out in couples therapy. The couples who stay together include three groups:

• The sufferers get stuck in the past, reliving it repeatedly and blaming each other for past misdemeanors. A relationship like this is an emotional prison and can hurt both partners much more than a breakup ever could.
• The builders stay together because commitment and the life they've built is their utmost priority. They have tremendous love and respect for each other, which helps them return to the earlier version of their relationship — the one before the affair. But if they and the relationship fail to change, the reasons the affair happened remain unaddressed. Therefore, the builders may be dooming themselves to repeat the same mistake.
• The explorers are the least common. These are the couples for whom infidelity was a trigger of significant transformation. For them, an affair becomes a new beginning. They don't revert to the previous version of themselves or their relationship. On the contrary, they work hard and explore new ways of commitment, building a second relationship from the ashes of the first.

A therapist’s job is to offer a couple a way out that won’t destroy their relationship. But while there are alternatives, it is essential to acknowledge that sometimes it is better and kinder to cut to the chase and end things.

A break-up isn’t always a failure; sometimes, it’s a way to mutual happiness.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
When faced with infidelity, romantic idealists (which most people are) often shield themselves from the notion that it could never happen to them. However pessimistic this may sound, there is no guarantee it won’t. I t doesn’t mean you must live with constant worry and suspicion that infidelity is just around the corner. Instead, it would be best to learn to coexist with many uncertainties, attractions, and allures — yours and your partner's. There’s no foolproof mechanism that will help prevent infidelity. That which is forbidden will always have a certain charm — it is part of human nature to chase it. But there are ways to strengthen relationships. Communicating, setting clear and explicit boundaries, and validating each other's freedom will bring you and your partner close r and make you less inclined to look for those things elsewhere.

The actual game changer can be a realization that even if affairs happen, they aren’t always the end of the world. Even though the hurt of betrayal is immeasurable, forgiveness is critical to freeing yourself from the claws of pain. Remember that your partner does not belong to you; you can lose them anytime. This knowledge shouldn't undermine the concept of commitment but drive you to work on the relationship and jolt you out of the mundane. After all, the love and security of a committed relationship are worth the fight.

Try this


• Talk through the definition of cheating with your partner; try to set clear boundaries of what is appropriate and inappropriate in your relationship.
• Don't be afraid to express your needs and plans to your other half; it will help determine if you are on the same page.
• Bring excitement into your relationship — plan date nights, go on trips, and try out each other's hobbies.

Read it till the end?
